title: Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic

learningObjectives.text: Explain primitive data types using a new example and justify the result.
Explain dynamic typing using a new example and justify the result.
Explain boolean values and logical reasoning using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: This topic turns values, data types, and boolean logic from isolated terminology into a practical tool for reading, writing, and reviewing code. It belongs to the **Orientation and Foundations** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Primitive data types** → **Dynamic typing** → **Boolean values and logical reasoning**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Primitive data types
sections.body: Primitive values include string, number, bigint, boolean, undefined, symbol, and null. They represent atomic values and are copied by value. Objects, arrays, and functions are reference-based values.

sections.title: Dynamic typing
sections.body: Variables are not permanently bound to one type. Each value still has a definite runtime type, but a variable may refer to different kinds of values over time. Clear data boundaries and validation are therefore important.

sections.title: Boolean values and logical reasoning
sections.body: Boolean reasoning combines propositions with NOT, AND, and OR. Translate requirements into smaller conditions, then inspect how short-circuiting affects both the result and which expressions execute.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: Each value has a runtime type even though variables are not permanently restricted to that type.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.
summary: Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic connects Primitive data types, Dynamic typing, Boolean values and logical reasoning.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains “Primitive data types” in the context of Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic?

choices.label: Primitive values include string, number, bigint, boolean, undefined, symbol, and null. They represent atomic values and are copied by value. Objects, arrays, and functions are reference-based values.
choices.feedback: Primitive data types is best understood as follows: Primitive values include string, number, bigint, boolean, undefined, symbol, and null. They represent atomic values and are copied by value. Objects, arrays, and functions are reference-based values.

choices.label: Objects can delegate missing property lookup to another object through a prototype link. Shared methods live on prototypes rather than being copied.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Primitive data types.

choices.label: Use a minimal failing case, logs, breakpoints, stack traces, and binary isolation to locate where observed behavior diverges from expected behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Primitive data types.

choices.label: Publisher–subscriber communication lets one part announce an event without directly knowing every consumer. Subscribers respond through a documented event and payload contract.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Primitive data types.

questions.explanation: Primitive data types is best understood as follows: Primitive values include string, number, bigint, boolean, undefined, symbol, and null. They represent atomic values and are copied by value. Objects, arrays, and functions are reference-based values.
questions.hint: Focus on what the concept changes, enables, or constrains—not only on its syntax.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic.

choices.label: Apply Primitive data types deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

choices.label: The statement is a misconception. A better approach is to reason from the concept's inputs, rules, and observable outcome, then verify the result.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic?

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic.

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Primitive data types, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic?

choices.label: Use Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Use Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic.

choices.label: Avoid Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Values, Data Types, and Boolean Logic.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
